passages.explanation: The fog-shrouded encounter between Dorian and Basil is laden with foreboding — the mist functions as a metaphor for the moral obscurity Dorian lives in, and Basil's emergence from it represents the return of conscience and accountability. Dorian's instinctive fear at seeing Basil, before any confrontation occurs, reveals that he already knows Basil represents a truth he cannot face. The detail of Basil's bag, packed for Paris, establishes the practical alibi that will later enable Dorian to conceal murder.

The casual banter about fog, trains, and servants creates a veneer of civility that masks the enormous tension beneath. Dorian's languid manner and insistence that 'nothing is serious nowadays' is his defense mechanism — turning everything into wit to avoid substance. Wilde contrasts Basil's earnest urgency with Dorian's studied indifference, establishing the fundamental conflict between those who believe in moral truth and those who aestheticize it away.

The cozy library setting — fire, lamps, silver spirit-case — creates an ironic backdrop for what will become a devastating moral confrontation. Dorian's elaborate small talk about servants and drinks is a strategy of deflection, filling the air with pleasantries to forestall Basil's serious purpose. His discomfort manifests as hospitality, a very Wildean observation about how social performance can function as emotional avoidance.

Basil's long speech cataloguing the ruin Dorian has brought on others is the novel's most direct moral indictment, and it carries special weight because it comes from someone who loves Dorian. Basil's belief that 'sin writes itself across a man's face' is the cruelest possible thing he could say to Dorian, though he means it as reassurance — it is precisely the supernatural exception to this rule that enables Dorian's double life. The litany of destroyed lives (the Guards boy, Adrian Singleton, the Duke of Perth) makes concrete what has been only hinted at, forcing both Dorian and the reader to confront the real human cost of his philosophy of sensation.

Dorian's furious defense — blaming the victims, invoking English hypocrisy, deflecting onto the middle classes — is a masterclass in moral evasion. His argument that he did not 'teach' anyone their vices echoes Lord Henry's earlier philosophy of influence without responsibility. Wilde forces us to ask: is the corruptor guilty, or are the corrupted? Basil's counter — that one can judge a man by his effect on his friends — cuts through the sophistry to a simple moral truth that Dorian cannot refute, only rage against.

The moment Basil says 'to see your soul,' Dorian's reaction — turning white with fear — reveals that beneath all his posturing, he knows exactly what he has become. His decision to show Basil the portrait is driven by 'the madness of pride' — a perverse need to be truly known, even if it destroys the witness. Wilde explores how the burden of a secret identity can become so great that exposure feels like relief, even when it carries terrible consequences. Dorian's desire to share his secret is simultaneously a confession and an act of aggression.

The final exchange before they ascend to the portrait room is charged with dramatic irony. Basil's faith that only God can see the soul is about to be shattered by the very artwork he created. Hallward's moment of pity — wondering what right he has to pry — represents the compassionate response Dorian can no longer access in himself. The painter's moral hesitation contrasts sharply with Dorian's cold certainty, marking the distance between a flawed but feeling human being and someone who has surrendered empathy for experience.
